title: A Good Opportunity

jokes.text: Murray calls band meeting roll call; Bret says 'Yes,' Jemaine says 'Also yes,' Murray says 'Present of course.'
jokes.setup: Murray runs a formal band meeting with a roll call, treating a two-man band like a corporate board.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's 'Also yes' and Murray's self-important 'Present of course' expose the absurd formality of their tiny, failing operation.
jokes.explanation: The formal roll call for a three-person operation (manager + two-man band) is a perfect encapsulation of the show's core joke: Murray's managerial delusion. 'Also yes' is a small, perfect Jemaine deadpan. The character integration is high — this gag only works because of these specific characters.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Your cds made double platinum in America... No, it's the crazy dogggz, sorry.

jokes.text: 'I thought we had sold five.' — Bret, on their CD sales.
jokes.setup: The moment of confusion about who made platinum sales.
jokes.punchline: Bret's resigned acknowledgment that they thought they'd sold five CDs total — delivered without self-pity.
jokes.explanation: The economy here is nearly perfect — five words that convey the entire tragic-comic situation of the band. The deadpan delivery is essential. It's a great throwaway that lands hard precisely because it's not played for sympathy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I thought we had sold five.

jokes.text: Murray announces R Kelly wants to sing on their next song — then realizes that's the Crazy Dogggz item too.
jokes.setup: Another exciting piece of news from Murray — R Kelly collaboration.
jokes.punchline: Again, it's for the Crazy Dogggz. The same bit repeated with escalating embarrassment for Murray.
jokes.explanation: The second iteration of the wrong-agenda gag. Works as a running joke but diminishes slightly from the first time. Still funny because R Kelly is a specifically absurd escalation — even bigger than double platinum.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They called me to say that you stole a cushion.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Did you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Murray defends his management: 'Before you came to me, you were poor and you had no gigs.' Bret: 'Now look at you.' Jemaine: 'We're poor and we've got no gigs.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to make the case that he's improved their situation.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine completes Murray's sentence perfectly honestly — their situation is identical to when they started.
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent. Murray sets up a self-defense that the boys complete for him — devastatingly. The rhythm is perfect: Murray's proud setup, Bret's neutral bridge ('Now look at you'), and Jemaine's flat completion that is simultaneously the punchline AND an honest observation. The joke works on multiple levels and is highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, this is a really nice cushion.

jokes.text: 'We're slightly poorer.' / 'Yeah, Bret's only got one shoe.'
jokes.setup: They've established they're still poor and have no gigs.
jokes.punchline: The escalation to 'slightly poorer' and the specific, absurd detail of Bret having one shoe as physical evidence of their decline.
jokes.explanation: The single shoe detail is comedy gold — specific, visual, and pathetic in exactly the right way. It also plants a running gag that pays off at the episode's end. 'Slightly poorer' as a qualifier to 'same situation' is a nice deadpan escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: R Kelly wants to sing on your next song... Yes, that's the crazy dogggz.

jokes.text: Item four on Murray's agenda: 'Stuff you.' Then 'Stuff you, Jemaine, and stuff you, Bret. And stuff you again, Jemaine.'
jokes.setup: Murray, after being fired, says he has one more agenda item.
jokes.punchline: The item is 'Stuff you' — delivered formally. Jemaine gets double-stuffed and asks why, which Murray doesn't explain.
jokes.explanation: This joke is exceptional because it uses the meeting structure as a comedic weapon — the formal 'Item four' framing makes the childish 'Stuff you' explosively funny. Then Jemaine's confused 'Why did I get double stuffed?' adds a perfect coda. Murray doesn't even acknowledge it, which is the right call. This is among the episode's best jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: dear Murray, We want to fire you as our manager.
What?
What?

jokes.text: 'I've got the Crazy Dogggz. They're making hit after hit. Doggy bounce, number one. Doggy dance, number five. In the pound, number 37.'
jokes.setup: Murray is defending himself and bragging about his other clients.
jokes.punchline: The song titles are hilariously on-the-nose dog puns, and the chart positions descend — number one, five, then 37 — a quietly deflating arc.
jokes.explanation: The descending chart positions are a subtle structural joke — Murray's pride is undercut by his own evidence. The song titles are absurdly literal in a way that fits the show's comic sensibility.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Now look at you.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We're poor and we've got no gigs.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We're slightly poorer.

jokes.text: Murray reveals the Gold records on the wall are fake — pencil sharpeners stuck to bits of wood — and the Grammys are fake too.
jokes.setup: Murray is bragging, gesturing at their Gold records on the wall.
jokes.punchline: He admits he made them himself — pencil sharpeners on wood — to make the boys feel better. Then the Grammys are fake. Then his 'Best Management' award is fake too.
jokes.explanation: This is a standout moment. The reveal escalates perfectly: fake Gold records → fake Grammys → fake 'Best Management' award (which Murray made for himself). That last detail — Murray fake-awarding himself — is a beautiful self-own that characterizes him perfectly. The 'pencil sharpeners stuck to bits of wood' specificity is wonderful. The escalation structure is pitch-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, bret's only got one shoe.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'I thought we won Best New Zealand Artists.' Murray: 'There's no such category, Jemaine.'
jokes.setup: Murray has revealed the Grammy was fake.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's specific memory of the award — 'Best New Zealand Artists' — is so niche and obviously non-existent that the denial lands perfectly.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Best New Zealand Artists' as a Grammy category is hilarious — it's so obviously not a real Grammy while being the exact kind of thing Jemaine would proudly believe he'd won. Murray's flat denial is perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I can get you another shoe...

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: it's not about the shoe...

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Not a problem. What size are you?

jokes.text: Murray closes the meeting: 'Band meeting finished... Forever.' — with a dramatic pause.
jokes.setup: The band meeting is over after the firing.
jokes.punchline: Murray's melodramatic 'Forever' after a beat — an overwrought close to a mundane meeting.
jokes.explanation: Murray's flair for the dramatic in mundane settings. The pause before 'Forever' is key — it makes his pettiness feel almost Shakespearean. Solid character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Item four: Stuff you.

jokes.text: Murray's parting shot: 'Good luck without me, you turkeys.'
jokes.setup: Murray exits after being fired.
jokes.punchline: 'You turkeys' — a mild, almost endearing insult that deflates what was meant to be a dramatic exit.
jokes.explanation: Murray's choice of 'turkeys' as his big dramatic insult is perfectly in character — he's trying to be cutting but lands somewhere between threatening and adorable. The specificity of the mild insult makes it funnier than anything harsher would be.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you sure that's not for the crazy dogggz?

jokes.text: Song lyric: 'Like a cake shop without any cakes'
jokes.setup: Murray's rejection similes continue.
jokes.punchline: A cake shop without cakes — an oddly specific, domestic, very non-rock comparison that perfectly captures Murray's New Zealand everyman sensibility.
jokes.explanation: This lyric is a highlight of the song section. The mundanity of the cake shop image is perfectly calibrated — it's not trying to be poetic or grand, just literally describing an empty shop, which captures Murray's limited but earnest imagination. The economy (six words, maximum absurdism) is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why did I get double stuffed?

jokes.text: Song lyric: 'Like a cornflake box without any flakes'
jokes.setup: Following the cake shop lyric.
jokes.punchline: The pattern continues — now an even more mundane breakfast cereal metaphor, escalating the domestic absurdity.
jokes.explanation: The second domestic simile escalates the joke by doubling down on mundanity. Cake shop → cornflake box is a funny step down in scale. The rhyme with 'cakes' is also neat. Works as part of the sequence rather than alone.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They're fake. I had to make them myself... They're pencil sharpeners stuck To a couple of bits of wood

jokes.text: Song lyric: 'Like a little orphan girl... who's been neglected' — with a long pause between the two lines.
jokes.setup: The song's similes have been mundane domestic objects; now Murray reaches for pathos.
jokes.punchline: He lands on 'a little orphan girl' — a melodramatic leap — then pauses for what must be several seconds before completing '...who's been neglected.'
jokes.explanation: The pause is the joke. The dramatic delay before a relatively mild completion ('who's been neglected') is a classic flight of the Conchords anti-climax. The pivot from cornflakes to orphan girls is also a funny tonal leap. The silence between lines is inferred from the timestamp gap (04:32 to 04:41).

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I thought we won 'best new zealand artists.'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: There's no such category, jemaine.

jokes.text: Song lyric: 'or bisected / slung, flung, tossed through / or trajected'
jokes.setup: The '-ected' rhyme scheme has been driving the song.
jokes.punchline: 'Trajected' — not a real word — is deployed with full confidence alongside 'bisected' (a real but extreme word) to maintain the rhyme. 'Slung, flung, tossed through' is wonderfully unnecessary.
jokes.explanation: 'Trajected' is a wonderfully confident fake word — the show's commitment to the rhyme scheme at the expense of real vocabulary is a meta-joke about Murray's limited education. The triple 'slung, flung, tossed through' is delightfully excessive.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'Doggy bounce,' number one. 'Doggy dance,' number five. 'In the pound,' number 37.

jokes.text: Murray's song is interrupted by Glen: 'There's a call for you from Poland... a lawyer.' Murray: 'Please, tell him I'm not there. I'm not here either. I'm not... not anywhere.'
jokes.setup: Murray is mid-song, deep in self-pity.
jokes.punchline: His instruction to deflect the call becomes an existential statement — 'I'm not anywhere' — which is both a plot point (he's hiding from legal trouble) and a beautiful expression of his emotional state.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best moments. The transition from the song's self-pity into mundane office business (a call from a Polish lawyer) is a perfect tonal collision. But Murray's escalation from 'tell him I'm not there' to 'I'm not here either' to 'I'm not... not anywhere' is genuinely poetic — it's simultaneously funny and a perfect expression of existential despair. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What about your 'best management' award? That's fake as well.

jokes.text: Murray at the boys' gig, watching a band: 'These guys are pretty good.' / 'They'll never hit the big time. They're too devious.'
jokes.setup: Murray apparently followed Bret and Jemaine to their gig and is watching another band.
jokes.punchline: His confident dismissal — 'too devious' — is a hilariously wrong-headed reason to predict failure. It suggests Murray's understanding of the music industry is bizarre.
jokes.explanation: 'Too devious' as a reason a band won't succeed is perfectly Murray — he has a strange, idiosyncratic internal logic that is never explained or questioned. A nice small character joke.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Good luck without me, you turkeys.

jokes.text: Murray shows up at the gig and tells the boys he used to do things 'behind the scenes' — things they couldn't see. Bret: 'What, when we couldn't see you?' / 'What would you be doing behind the scenes? Having dinner? Going to the movies? Relaxing?'
jokes.setup: Murray claims he did important management work 'behind the scenes.'
jokes.punchline: The boys' sincere guesses — 'having dinner,' 'going to the movies,' 'relaxing' — suggest they genuinely think this is what he was doing, which is probably accurate.
jokes.explanation: The boys' deadpan guesses hit exactly right — they're not being sarcastic, they genuinely think this is plausible, which makes the joke land both as character comedy and as an indictment of Murray's work ethic. 'Relaxing?' especially is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ rejected ♪ ♪ thrown away ♪ ♪ affected ♪ ♪ I don't know what to say ♪

jokes.text: Murray: 'Photocopying. Haggling.' Jemaine: 'Higgling?' Murray: 'Yeah, do you know what that means?' Jemaine: 'Is that a word that you just made up?' Murray: 'It's made-up, yeah, but...'
jokes.setup: Murray lists his management activities, including 'higgling.'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately identifies 'higgling' as a made-up word; Murray confirms it is made-up but doesn't retract it or seem embarrassed — he just moves on.
jokes.explanation: The exchange is a masterclass in the show's deadpan rhythm. Murray invents a word, is called out immediately, admits it's made up, and doesn't apologize or explain — just trails off with 'but...' The confidence-without-self-awareness is peak Murray. The word 'higgling' is also genuinely funny as a made-up management term.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ like a cake shop without any cakes ♪ ♪ like a cornflake box without any flakes ♪

jokes.text: Emergency band meeting during the conversation with the agency executives. Murray: 'No, please, go on. Shoot.' Boys run off anyway. Martin Clark: 'Don't listen.'
jokes.setup: Martin Clark wants to offer them a jingle deal right there. Murray insists on an emergency band meeting.
jokes.punchline: The boys literally walk away from the executives mid-conversation for a private meeting. Murray says 'don't listen' to the executives — who are standing three feet away.
jokes.explanation: The band meeting ritual transposed into a professional setting creates good cringe comedy. Murray telling the executives not to listen is a lovely absurd beat. The cringe of walking away from someone mid-pitch is very FotC.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ like a little orphan girl... ♪
Murray?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: One moment.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: ♪ and rejected. ♪

jokes.text: Emergency band meeting within earshot: Item one is band merchandise — a t-shirt with 'Flight of the Conchords' written in tiny letters in pen. 'But we can't sell it tonight cause I'm wearing it.'
jokes.setup: The emergency band meeting is ostensibly about the jingle deal.
jokes.punchline: Item one is a t-shirt Bret made — with the band name written in tiny pen letters — that they can't sell because he's wearing it.
jokes.explanation: A perfect encapsulation of the band's hopeless DIY approach. The specific details accumulate beautifully: self-made, tiny letters, in pen, currently worn. Each qualifier makes it funnier. This is the kind of mundane-absurdist detail the show excels at.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: One moment.
and rejected.

dialogue.speaker: Glen
dialogue.line: Yes, Murray?

jokes.text: Bret made band dolls — with a real denim jacket on one. 'How'd you get the jacket so realistic?' 'Yeah, that's real denim.'
jokes.setup: Bret reveals band merchandise including hand-made dolls.
jokes.punchline: The doll's jacket is made from real denim — Bret apparently cut up actual denim to make a tiny jacket for a doll nobody will buy.
jokes.explanation: The real-denim detail on a handmade doll is a lovely absurdist touch. Bret's commitment to authenticity in a completely pointless direction (tiny doll jacket) is very in-character. Also inferred as a visual gag — we'd see the small handmade doll onscreen.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Please, tell him I'm not there. I'm not here either. I'm not... not anywhere.

jokes.text: Jemaine apparently tries to take some of Bret's hair for the doll mid-meeting: 'And I just want to get some of your hair...' before being interrupted.
jokes.setup: The doll conversation is interrupted by the next agenda item.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is apparently in the middle of physically harvesting Bret's hair for the doll, mid-emergency meeting, in front of executives.
jokes.explanation: The visual of Jemaine reaching for Bret's hair in the middle of a professional opportunity, with executives watching, is peak cringe-absurdist comedy. The interruption is the right call — the implied action is funnier than any completion.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They'll never hit the big time. They're too devious. Trust me, though, they're devious. I know.

jokes.text: Bret to Martin Clark about his email: 'Underscore is your middle name, is it?' — re: martinóclark@aol.com
jokes.setup: Martin Clark gives his email address: martinóclark@aol.com
jokes.punchline: Bret deadpans that the underscore must be Clark's middle name — a literal, slow-brained interpretation of an email address.
jokes.explanation: A nice small deadpan moment. Bret's literal reading of the email format as biographical data is in character — slightly naive, oddly specific. Also a gentle AOL joke (AOL email in the professional world already dates him).

dialogue.line: What would you be doing behind the scenes?
Having dinner?
Going to the movies?
Relaxing?

jokes.text: Martin: 'I need a demo by the end of the week. Cool?' Bret: 'Yes.' Jemaine: 'Impossible. Oh. Yes.'
jokes.setup: Martin sets a deadline.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine starts to object ('Impossible'), catches himself, then agrees. The three-beat response — certainty, correction, capitulation — is perfectly deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Economy is excellent — the entire joke is in the rhythm of 'Impossible. Oh. Yes.' A tiny moment but very well-constructed. Jemaine's automatic truth-telling followed by self-correction is a good character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Um, higgling.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Higgling?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, do you know what that means?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Is that a word that you just made up?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's made-up, yeah

jokes.text: First attempt at femident jingle: '¶ Some women like men / some are lesbian / Femident toothpaste. ¶' — three lines, full stop.
jokes.setup: The boys try to figure out what women like to write a toothpaste jingle for women.
jokes.punchline: Their entire jingle is three lines: an observation about women's sexuality (delivered neutrally), a brand mention. It's both absurdly incomplete and somehow correct in its logic.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The jingle is hilariously minimal and logically incoherent — they've listed one heterosexual option and one homosexual option and concluded the brand name is sufficient. The delivery is clearly deadpan and matter-of-fact. The economy (three lines to convey maximum absurdity) is almost perfect. Highly quotable, original, and very FotC.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: This could make you so rich you'll be shitting money. Literally if you wanted.

jokes.text: Bret: 'It's... there's almost half.' / 'That's half of it.' — on the three-line jingle.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is unimpressed with the first jingle attempt.
jokes.punchline: Bret's honest assessment: 'there's almost half' of a jingle there — delivered with genuine optimism.
jokes.explanation: The mathematical optimism of 'almost half' is a nice Bret character beat — he's not wrong that three lines is a start, but calling it 'almost half' of something is aspirationally delusional. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But we can't sell it tonight 'cause I'm wearing it.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Women love weaving. They love to weave.' Bret: 'Nah, weaving is a man's game.'
jokes.setup: They're brainstorming what women like for the jingle.
jokes.punchline: Bret confidently declares weaving is 'a man's game' — a completely arbitrary, unsupported gender claim delivered with total conviction.
jokes.explanation: The randomness of weaving as a women's interest and Bret's immediate counter-claim are both funny. Neither is defensible. The deadpan certainty on both sides is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How'd you get the jacket so realistic?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's real denim.

jokes.text: Bret: 'I come from a family of weavers.' / 'I'm weaving at the moment, making a pair of trousers.'
jokes.setup: Bret is defending the masculinity of weaving with personal evidence.
jokes.punchline: He's currently weaving a pair of trousers — so committed to proving it's a man's game that he's doing it himself, as a grown man in New York.
jokes.explanation: A good extension of the weaving bit. The specificity of 'making a pair of trousers' is charming — it's not a blanket or a scarf (which might be expected from a weaver). Bret's commitment to the argument via personal practice is a nice character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: here's two men Who know exactly what women feel like.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Women's rights.' Bret: 'No, that's more of a man's thing, isn't it?' — said without irony.
jokes.setup: They're listing things women like for the jingle.
jokes.punchline: Bret genuinely believes women's rights is 'more of a man's thing' — not as a political statement but as a literal genre confusion.
jokes.explanation: A clean, sharp joke. The irony works because Bret is clearly not being misogynistic — he's just genuinely confused about categories, which makes it funnier. The matter-of-fact delivery is key. Quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Underscore is your middle name, is it?

jokes.text: Dave: 'No, these women I met, I took them up on a plane, they were making a salad, next thing you know, they're just wrestling at 85,000 feet.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine called Dave's claim a ridiculous generalization. Dave defends it.
jokes.punchline: Dave's 'evidence' is an anecdote about taking women on a plane where they were making a salad — that led to oil wrestling at 85,000 feet (above commercial airspace).
jokes.explanation: The escalation of detail — salad → wrestling, then the altitude of 85,000 feet (twice as high as any commercial plane flies) stated as a specific but impossible fact — is excellent. Dave's complete sincerity throughout makes it funnier. The '85,000 feet' detail is a lovely absurdist precision.

dialogue.line: ♪ some women like men ♪
♪ some are lesbian ♪
♪ femident toothpaste. ♪

jokes.text: Dave: 'You said there would be a lot of women here today.' / 'They're probably just doing brazilians. They'll show up.'
jokes.setup: Dave promised women would be at the hardware store.
jokes.punchline: His explanation for the absence — they're 'doing brazilians' — is both a slightly crude non-sequitur and an oddly confident, specific theory.
jokes.explanation: A functional joke — 'doing brazilians' as Dave's explanation is in character but slightly lazy compared to the better Dave jokes in this scene. 'They'll show up' is a nice confident coda.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Nah. Weaving is a man's game... I come from a family of weavers.

jokes.text: Boys tell Murray 'It's easier than easy' / 'Easier than you thought.' Murray: 'Oh okay, so you're telling me it was unbelievably easy.'
jokes.setup: The boys wanted to hurt Murray by telling him they don't need him.
jokes.punchline: Murray turns their gloating into a precise escalating question — 'unbelievably easy' — which actually causes him more pain, delivered with his characteristic precise diction.
jokes.explanation: The deadpan escalation of synonyms (easy → easier than easy → unbelievably easy) is a nice small bit. Murray's precise articulation of his own humiliation is good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I thought your dad was a sheep lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, during the day, but at night he weaves a lot.

jokes.text: Murray warns: 'I've been to Bangkok. Now Monday you're trying to do a TV commercial, they're filming it, it's all about toothpaste, then Tuesday they're trying to film you naked.'
jokes.setup: Murray is warning them about the dangers of doing advertising work without a manager.
jokes.punchline: His warning escalates from toothpaste ad to naked filming to drugs, all as a logical inevitability — and his authority for this is 'I've been to Bangkok.'
jokes.explanation: Murray's Bangkok credential as authority for his completely unhinged advertising industry warning is perfect character comedy. The Monday-to-Tuesday timeline for toothpaste → naked filming is a nice compressed absurdist escalation. 'Happens all the time, guys' seals it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Women's rights.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, that's more of a man's thing, isn't it? ... my father's a women's rights activist. Dad wouldn't allow that.

jokes.text: Murray plays them the Crazy Dogggz song and a Polish song that turns out to be identical — and came out 13 years earlier.
jokes.setup: Murray asks them to listen to two songs and give their 'professional opinions.'
jokes.punchline: The songs are identical; the Crazy Dogggz 'original' is a copy of a Polish song from 13 years earlier. Murray asks if that's 'bad or normal.'
jokes.explanation: The plot mechanics are funny (Murray's big clients are plagiarists) but the funnier beat is his frame — asking them if plagiarism is 'bad or normal' as if it might be a gray area. This sets up the next bit.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: They like wrestling in cooking oil on a plane... I took them up on a plane, they were making a salad, Next thing you know, they're just wrestling at 85,000 feet.

jokes.text: Murray tallying up 'bads' and 'normals' on the plagiarism question, getting confused about who said what: 'So you're normal... No, I'm bad... I've got three...'
jokes.setup: Murray is polling whether plagiarism 13 years earlier is 'bad or normal.'
jokes.punchline: His tally system breaks down — he mishears answers, gets confused between respondents, and is interrupted mid-count by movers.
jokes.explanation: Functionally funny — Murray's bureaucratic approach to a moral question (polling it) and then messing up the poll is good character comedy. Not the sharpest bit but earns its place.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: They're probably just doing brazilians. They'll show up. Don't worry.

jokes.text: Movers start removing furniture from Murray's office mid-meeting. Murray: 'Excuse me. Careful with that. That's worth $20,000. It was a gift from Tori Amos.'
jokes.setup: Murray's office is being cleared out while he's trying to run a meeting.
jokes.punchline: He pauses to claim something is worth $20,000 and was a gift from Tori Amos — then calmly returns to the meeting.
jokes.explanation: Tori Amos as the specific celebrity whose gift Murray treasures is a perfectly arbitrary choice — enough to be absurd but not so outlandish as to seem random. The $20,000 valuation suggests either it's actually valuable (and Murray has some real connections) or he's lying (more likely).

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's easier than easy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, okay, so you're telling me It was unbelievably easy.

jokes.text: Boys: 'Are you moving office, Murray?' Murray: 'I'm kind of moving.' Item three: 'Would any of you like to buy a desk?'
jokes.setup: Murray's office is being dismantled around him.
jokes.punchline: He frames his eviction as 'kind of moving' and repurposes the band meeting to sell office furniture.
jokes.explanation: Murray's use of the formal meeting structure to sell his furniture during his eviction is a great character beat. 'I'm kind of moving' as understatement for being evicted is excellent deadpan. Agenda 'item three' being 'would you like to buy a desk' is a beautiful comedic structure payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Monday you're trying to do a TV commercial, They're filming it, it's all about toothpaste, Then Tuesday they're filming you naked.

jokes.text: Outside, protesters appear holding a sign: 'Crazy Dogggz, we're onto you! You ruined his life!'
jokes.setup: The plagiarism controversy is just coming to light.
jokes.punchline: Mel and Doug (the superfan couple) appear outside Murray's office building protesting the Crazy Dogggz — they've mobilized for the band's indirect benefit, unprompted.
jokes.explanation: Good use of the superfan characters. 'You ruined his life!' is an endearingly dramatic statement for a band none of them are even in. Their commitment to protecting the boys is the ongoing character joke.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: This cover version came out 13 years Before the crazy dogggz's original version.

jokes.text: Mel calls Doug a 'conversation hog,' doing 'oink oink oink' sounds — but Doug has barely spoken.
jokes.setup: Doug apparently tried to say something about the Crazy Dogggz situation.
jokes.punchline: Mel furiously accuses him of hogging the conversation and oinks at him — but from context, Doug has said almost nothing.
jokes.explanation: Mel's domestic tyranny over Doug is an established FotC running gag. The irony of accusing someone who has barely spoken of hogging the conversation is good. The oink noises are a nice physical/absurdist touch.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: So I've got... so you're normal...

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I'm bad.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're bad?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've got three...

jokes.text: Dave on the jingle: 'Some of the best songs are jingles. ¶ Pussymart, Pussymart. ¶'
jokes.setup: Bret asks Dave if they're selling out by doing a jingle.
jokes.punchline: Dave illustrates his point by singing two words of an apparent jingle — 'Pussymart' — and stops there as if that's a known classic.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — a single two-word jingle dropped completely casually as an example of great song writing. 'Pussymart' is a hilariously unsexy portmanteau that sounds vaguely adult and yet is delivered as if it's Beethoven. The 'I don't know that one' response confirms nobody knows it. Highly quotable, perfectly absurd, excellent Dave character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's worth $20,000. It was a gift from Tori Amos.

jokes.text: Dave demonstrates the 'Dave double-down technique': 'How much would you give me for this? / A dollar. / Double it. / $2. / Double it again. / $4. / Sold!'
jokes.setup: Dave claims to be a master negotiator and offers to demonstrate his technique.
jokes.punchline: His 'technique' is just asking someone to double their offer twice — resulting in $4 for something that started at $1, which he presents as a triumph.
jokes.explanation: The negotiation demo is beautifully structured. Dave's confidence in a technique that only gets him $4 total, which he presents as brilliance, is great. The structure is crisp — the escalating doubles feel mathematical and logical until the punchline of 'Sold!' at $4. Then Bret's confused 'What's go... what?' before Dave explains he 'made him pay nearly 10 times what he wanted' (the math is also wrong) seals it.

dialogue.line: You should've seen Doug When he heard what happened.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Hey.
He went berserk.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Really?
Actually, mel really went more sort of berserk.

jokes.text: Dave: 'There's your pen. Just give me my $4.' Bret: 'I thought it was an exercise.' Dave: 'Yeah, you've got to learn the whole lesson.' ... 'I'm gonna waive the tax.'
jokes.setup: Bret thought the negotiation demo was hypothetical.
jokes.punchline: Dave insists the $4 is real money — he actually sold whatever the item was. Then generously waives the tax.
jokes.explanation: A good coda to the negotiation bit. 'You've got to learn the whole lesson' as justification for actually taking the money is perfect Dave logic. The tax waiver is a generous final touch from a man who just scammed $4 from a friend.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Conversation hog. 'Oink oink oink oink. I've got something to say all the time.' He's always talking.

jokes.text: Martin: 'I know you're from New Zealand. What is this, Lord of the Rings? We don't have all day.'
jokes.setup: The 18-minute jingle has just been revealed.
jokes.punchline: Martin's comparison to Lord of the Rings as a New Zealand cultural reference for 'things that are too long' is a pointed but fair jab.
jokes.explanation: A functional but somewhat expected joke — LOTR/New Zealand is not a fresh observation. Delivered well but not especially original.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Why did you come, Doug, If you didn't want to light a fire?

jokes.text: Caitlin: 'Some of them aren't about toothpaste, they're just things in our life.'
jokes.setup: The jingle lyrics are being critiqued.
jokes.punchline: The admission that the jingle contains 'just things in our life' — the boys literally padded an 18-minute toothpaste jingle with personal musings.
jokes.explanation: Good character joke — the band's inability to separate art from their lived experience is very them. The image of an 18-minute jingle full of personal journaling is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Some of the best songs are jingles. ♪ pussymart, pussymart. ♪

jokes.text: Bret tries to pre-emptively negotiate ('We were wondering if you could double it') before Martin has named the fee, then has to unsay it.
jokes.setup: Martin says he has news about the fee.
jokes.punchline: Bret jumps in with 'double it' before the fee is announced — applying Dave's technique at the wrong moment. Then the fee is revealed at $1,000 each, which Martin had already doubled internally from $500.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured callback to Dave's negotiation lesson. Bret attempts the technique at the wrong moment, then the punchline reveals Martin had already pre-doubled it — so Bret's negotiation achieved nothing. 'He already doubled it in his mind' is a great coda. Excellent callback payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: The d in my name stands for 'deal.' ... the word 'Dave' Actually means 'deal' in latino?

jokes.text: Murray reveals he's living in his car — but denies it immediately: 'No, of course not. It's illegal. You can't do that... Apparently... Unless you move your vehicle every three hours.'
jokes.setup: Boys ask 'Are you living in your car, Murray?'
jokes.punchline: Murray's denial immediately collapses — he knows the specific legal rule about moving the vehicle every three hours, which only someone living in their car would know.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's very best jokes. The structure is perfect: confident denial → legal caveat → specific knowledge that completely undermines the denial. Murray knows the exact rule because he's been doing it. The pacing — 'No, of course not. It's illegal. You can't do that... Apparently... Unless you move your vehicle every three hours' — is metronomic comedy. Deeply character-specific, beautifully structured, and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: How much would you give me for this?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: A dollar.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Double it...

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sold!

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I just made him pay Nearly 10 times what he wanted to pay

jokes.text: Murray is clearly living in his car, treating his glovebox as a 'stationery cabinet.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks Bret to get a pencil from his 'stationery cabinet.'
jokes.punchline: The 'stationery cabinet' is the glove compartment of his car — euphemism as a coping mechanism.
jokes.explanation: Murray renaming his glovebox a 'stationery cabinet' is a lovely bit of tragic dignity. He's maintaining professional airs while clearly living out of his car. The visual of Bret opening a glovebox to find organized stationery is implied and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I thought it was an exercise.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Yeah, you've got to learn the whole lesson.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Why don't you get your old job back at the consulate?' Murray: 'I can't do that, Bret. I've moved on. Look at me. I'm not going back there. Plus when I quit I told them all to go stuff themselves.' / 'No, I wrote it down, but it's the same feeling. Imagine reading that.'
jokes.setup: Bret suggests Murray just go back to his old safe job.
jokes.punchline: Murray's principled stand collapses when pressed — he told them to 'stuff themselves' but only in a letter. His defense: 'it's the same feeling. Imagine reading that.'
jokes.explanation: Murray's written-not-spoken grand gesture is perfectly in character — he wanted the dramatic exit but chickened out of delivering it in person. 'Imagine reading that' as a defense of written vs. spoken confrontation is endearingly pathetic and very Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: This song is 18 minutes long. We're talking about a 30-second commercial here.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I'm persona non regates.' / 'You're not at yacht race?'
jokes.setup: Murray uses the phrase 'persona non grata.'
jokes.punchline: He mispronounces it as 'persona non regates'; Jemaine hears 'regattas' and asks if he's been excluded from a yacht race.
jokes.explanation: This is a great wordplay/mishearing joke. Murray's malapropism creates a genuine misunderstanding, and Jemaine's yacht race interpretation is the perfect wrong turn. It works on multiple levels: Murray's limited grasp of Latin phrases, Jemaine's literal parsing, and the incongruity of yacht races in this context. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We thought it would be more of a concept.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: I know you're from new zealand. What is this, 'lord of the rings'?

jokes.text: Murray kicks them out of the car: 'I've got to go. That's enough. Thank you. Out you get. Gone. Both of you.' Jemaine: 'There wasn't a meeting, Murray. It doesn't count as a meeting.'
jokes.setup: The car conversation has gone on long enough for Murray.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's final word: despite everything, he needs Murray to know it wasn't a meeting — protecting the sanctity of the band meeting format.
jokes.explanation: A good callback to the episode's opening band meeting bit. Jemaine's insistence that an informal car conversation doesn't count as a meeting shows both characters' shared investment in meaningless procedural formality. The irony of policing meeting protocols with a manager who just lost his office is good.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: some of them aren't about toothpaste, They're just things in our life.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Nice pants.' Bret: 'Are they woven? Wow.' / 'These are man-made.'
jokes.setup: Murray compliments Bret's pants as they leave.
jokes.punchline: The weaving debate from earlier resurfaces — Bret's pants might be woven, but they're 'man-made,' which in the earlier argument actually supports his position.
jokes.explanation: A nice light callback to the weaving debate. 'Man-made' is an ambiguous term that could support Bret's earlier claim. Low-key but pleasant.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: The fee is $1,000 each.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sorry, now I'll say it. Double it.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: I am doubling it. It was going to be $500 each.

jokes.text: The Femident toothpaste jingle song performed in the commercial — 'You are a woman, you wear women's wear / you have breasts and longish hair, oh yeah / you're kind of fun everywhere / yet you're still very rare, oh yeah'
jokes.setup: The commercial jingle that was supposed to speak to women.
jokes.punchline: The lyrics are hilariously literal and condescending — describing a woman as having 'longish hair' and being 'kind of fun everywhere' and 'still very rare' — none of these are things women want to hear about themselves.
jokes.explanation: The full jingle is a sustained piece of well-constructed comedy. 'Longish hair' and 'kind of fun everywhere' as defining female attributes are perfect — specific enough to seem thought-out, wrong enough to be hilarious. 'Yet you're still very rare' is especially odd — what does that mean? The boys' complete sincerity is what makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: I am doubling it. It was going to be $500 each.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He already doubled it in his mind.

jokes.text: Jingle lyric: 'You're a woman and you love to weave / you're a woman, you have women's needs / I know you love women's rights / you're a woman with teeth, now take a bite'
jokes.setup: The jingle continues.
jokes.punchline: All their earlier brainstorming (weaving, women's rights, teeth) made it into the song — including the specific items from their ridiculous brainstorm session. 'You're a woman with teeth' as a selling point is especially absurd.
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent callback payoff. Every absurd item from their brainstorm (weaving, women's rights) appeared in the final song — the episode's middle section set up a payoff that lands perfectly. 'You're a woman with teeth' is the standout line — it's technically accurate, utterly unhelpful as a selling point, and a perfect comedic non-sequitur. The callback structure elevates this above standard joke fare.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's the glovebox there.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ah. Oh.

jokes.text: After filming the commercial, the ad exec asks for work permits. Boys: 'We don't have work permits.' / 'Is it just a bit of card? Just a card, a greenish card? Is it like a library card?'
jokes.setup: The ad exec discovers they might be working illegally and asks about green cards.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's (or Bret's) attempts to identify what a green card might be — 'a greenish card,' 'like a library card' — reveal complete ignorance of American immigration law.
jokes.explanation: The incremental, sincere attempts to identify what a green card is ('just a bit of card?' / 'a greenish card?') are very in character — naive and literal. The final reveal that they're potentially illegal immigrants is a good escalation of their ongoing failure.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm persona non regates.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You know what that means?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're not at yacht race?

jokes.text: Bret: 'I don't think we're legally allowed to be in the country.' Jemaine: 'No, we're illegal immigrants.'
jokes.setup: The work permit conversation is escalating.
jokes.punchline: Bret's tentative 'I don't think we're legally allowed to be in the country' followed by Jemaine's matter-of-fact confirmation — 'No, we're illegal immigrants' — delivered as casual information.
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of their illegal status is perfectly calibrated — they say it as if it's a minor administrative oversight rather than a serious crime. 'No, we're illegal immigrants' as a calm statement of fact is a great comedy beat.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm persona non regates.
You know what that means?
No.
You're not at yacht race?

jokes.text: Ad exec's reaction: 'You bastards better bring your passports in and show us your goddamn work permits!'
jokes.setup: The boys have calmly revealed they might be illegal immigrants.
jokes.punchline: The exec's disproportionate anger — going from professional to 'you bastards' and 'goddamn' in seconds — is the reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: The exec's eruption is a good reaction beat but less interesting than the boys' deadpan reveal. Functional comedy that earns a laugh but isn't the episode's strongest material.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Unless you move your vehicle every three hours.

jokes.text: Bret phones Murray: 'We've got blue robes on. We're not wearing anything...' Murray: 'Get dressed.'
jokes.setup: Bret is explaining their situation to Murray on the phone.
jokes.punchline: Bret mentions they're wearing blue robes (from the commercial) and starts to explain they're not wearing anything — implying nudity under the robes — and Murray immediately cuts him off.
jokes.explanation: Murray's Bangkok warning foreshadowed nudity in commercial work — this is a mild callback. The visual of them in robes calling their manager for help is funny. Not a standout moment but solid.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Are they woven? Wow.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: These are man-made.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Can you bring our passports and some green cards, please?'
jokes.setup: They're in legal trouble for not having work permits.
jokes.punchline: Bret asks Murray to bring 'some green cards' as if they're something Murray might have lying around — treating a multi-year immigration process as a simple errand.
jokes.explanation: Bret asking Murray to bring 'some green cards' as if they're a stationery item treats a complex immigration document as a casual request. Good callback to their earlier confusion about what a green card is.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: ♪ you are a woman, you wear women's wear ♪

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: ♪ you have breasts and longish hair, oh yeah ♪

jokes.text: Greg ignores Murray's emotional speech and reads him phone messages instead: 'One from May... something about your gym membership expiring... One from the Prime Minister from September / And one just before Christma... / No, that's... that was a wrong number.'
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to deliver a heartfelt apology. Greg keeps interrupting with administrative updates.
jokes.punchline: Greg's messages escalate absurdly — gym membership → Prime Minister → something from before Christmas (tantalizingly cut off) — and the Prime Minister message from September apparently wasn't urgent enough to relay. Then the final 'wrong number' reveal deflates everything.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke. Greg using Murray's apology speech as an opportunity to relay old messages is a perfect comedic inversion — the emotional beats are repeatedly interrupted by mundane admin. The escalation from gym membership to the Prime Minister is perfectly paced, and the final 'wrong number' undercut is a wonderful anti-climax. The Christmas one being cut off suggests something juicy — a classic withheld punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is it just a bit of card? ... Just a card, a greenish card? ... Is it like a library card?

jokes.text: Murray returns victorious: 'Well, good news, guys: I got my job back, I got your passports. Bad news, guys: Your passports aren't ready. They're not processed.'
jokes.setup: Murray announces good news.
jokes.punchline: Immediately negates himself — the good news (got passports) is instantly retracted (passports aren't ready/processed). The structure perfectly mirrors Murray's typical management — appearing helpful while achieving nothing.
jokes.explanation: The good news/bad news structure works perfectly here because the bad news directly negates the good news in the same breath. It's the Murray management philosophy in one exchange: announce a solution, undercut it immediately. Good structural symmetry.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, we're illegal immigrants.

jokes.text: Murray uses their legal crisis as leverage: 'If you hire me back, I'll tell you what to do.' They rehire him. 'What should we do?' Murray: 'I think we should run for it. Come on, let's go.'
jokes.setup: Murray has been maneuvering to get rehired. He finally extracts a yes, then is asked for his expert advice.
jokes.punchline: His expert managerial advice is simply 'run for it.' The big buildup to rehiring results in the most amateur possible solution.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the ceremony of rehiring and the triviality of the advice ('run for it') is a strong comedic beat. Murray's advice is always slightly worse than no advice — here it's literal flight. Good character resolution.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We've got blue robes on. We're not wearing anything...

jokes.text: After running to Murray's car, Murray: 'Good managing, eh, guys?' — while they're all clearly in distress/fleeing.
jokes.setup: They've just fled a legal situation by literally running.
jokes.punchline: Murray's self-congratulatory 'good managing, eh, guys?' after their solution was just running away is a perfect expression of his delusional self-assessment.
jokes.explanation: Murray's pride in running away as management strategy is a perfectly in-character moment. The self-congratulation after a non-solution is deeply Murray. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Bad news, guys: Your passports aren't ready.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you have them?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, they're in my cupboard.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You just said you had them.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've got them in my cupboard.

jokes.text: Murray produces a shoe for Bret: 'Oh, Bret, that's for you.' / 'I managed to get you a shoe, eh?' / 'That's okay. You know what? I think there's a guardian angel looking after us.'
jokes.setup: The episode ends with them fleeing legal trouble.
jokes.punchline: Murray has somehow procured a single shoe for Bret (solving the one-shoe problem from the opening) — and presents this as evidence of guardian angels. This after completely failing at everything else.
jokes.explanation: An excellent full-circle callback. Murray resolves the one thing he fixated on (the shoe) while failing at everything else — and interprets this as divinely arranged. His attribution of mundane shoe procurement to a 'guardian angel' while they're fleeing law enforcement is a perfect final character statement. The structure — episode starts with one shoe, ends with a shoe delivered triumphantly — is very satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: This is exactly what happens when you try to manage yourselves. If you had stuck with me, you wouldn't be in this job situation, would you?

jokes.text: Murray: 'I think there's a guardian angel looking after us. I'm sure of it.' Jemaine: 'Murray, it couldn't have possibly gone worse.'
jokes.setup: Murray is attributing their misadventures to divine protection.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's flat correction — 'it couldn't have possibly gone worse' — while Murray continues to believe they're blessed.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Murray's optimism and the objective reality is the show's central joke — they've failed at everything and Murray thinks they're protected by angels. Jemaine's deadpan is perfectly calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I think we should run for it. Come on, let's go. Go! Run! Run! Quickly! No time for debate!

jokes.text: Song lyric: 'Nobody knows what goes on / under those robes'
jokes.setup: The angels song.
jokes.punchline: The robes line implies the song has considered angel anatomy/underwear — and the mystery is invoked as a kind of celestial forbidden knowledge.
jokes.explanation: A standout line in the song. 'Under those robes' is a genuinely funny piece of celestial speculation — are angels commando under there? The delivery as a mysterious, almost reverent observation elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I managed to get you a shoe, eh?

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine fire Murray for neglecting them in favour of the Crazy Dogggz and almost immediately they receive an offer to write a jingle for a commercial. Meanwhile Murray's fortunes reverse after he discovers that the Crazy Dogggz big hit is a rip-off of a 1990s Polish song.
